Experiment_description: Preliminary implementation of Phase-Aware Data Attribution (PADA) for dynamic training data selection, tested on synthetic sequence prediction tasks with small transformer models. PADA implements three components: (1) gradient-norm-based sample attribution, (2) validation-loss-dynamics-based phase detection classifying training into memorization/generalization/refinement phases, and (3) phase-conditioned sampling prioritizing different sample types per phase. Compared against random selection and full-data training baselines across multiple independent runs.
Significance: This preliminary stage reveals critical implementation challenges for phase-aware data selection methods. The findings demonstrate that naive implementation of dynamic attribution-based sampling does not automatically yield efficiency gains, highlighting the importance of proper phase detection calibration, sampling budget enforcement, and training stability. These results provide diagnostic insights for developing more robust curriculum learning and data selection approaches.
Description: Multiple nodes implemented PADA with slight variations on synthetic text/sequence data using small transformer models (GPT-style) for next-token prediction. The phase detector tracked validation loss dynamics to classify training into three phases: memorization (rapid loss drop), generalization (steady improvement), and refinement (slow convergence/plateau). The sampler was designed to prioritize high-gradient diverse samples during memorization, boundary cases during generalization, and high-uncertainty samples during refinement. Training ran for 20-30 epochs with sample efficiency ratio (validation loss improvement per sample used) as the primary metric. Baselines included random subset selection and full-data training.

List_of_included_plots.description: Validation loss vs total samples used showing PADA, Random, and Full Data methods
List_of_included_plots.analysis: Critical plot for evaluating sample efficiency claims. Shows that when controlling for sample count, PADA does not dominate the Pareto frontier - it achieves roughly equal or slightly worse validation loss than Random selection at comparable sample budgets. Full Data continues improving with more samples. This contradicts the core hypothesis that phase-aware attribution should provide better samples than random selection.

List_of_included_plots.description: Phase distribution bar chart showing epochs spent in each phase: memorization=5, generalization=15, refinement=0
List_of_included_plots.analysis: Reveals a critical failure mode: the phase detector never triggers the refinement phase across 20 epochs of training. Without late-phase switching, PADA cannot apply refinement-specific sampling criteria (uncertainty-focused selection), effectively collapsing to a two-phase system. This indicates phase detection thresholds need calibration based on validation loss plateau detection or alternative metrics.

List_of_included_plots.description: Summary figure showing validation loss, sample efficiency, cumulative samples, and phase distribution for an alternative PADA implementation
List_of_included_plots.analysis: Shows contrasting failure mode where PADA uses substantially MORE samples (88k vs 62k for random) while achieving worse validation loss. Training loss exhibits dramatic oscillations (dips and spikes) indicating instability from distribution shift caused by aggressive sample selection changes. The phase distribution here shows 18/30 epochs in refinement (opposite problem from other nodes), suggesting phase detection is highly sensitive to hyperparameters.

List_of_included_plots.description: Training loss over epochs comparing PADA, Random Selection, and Full Data
List_of_included_plots.analysis: PADA shows consistently higher training loss with more oscillation compared to baselines. This indicates PADA is not merely overfitting less - it is underfitting due to reduced/poorer data exposure. The oscillations suggest the changing sample distribution between epochs creates optimization instability. Higher training loss combined with higher validation loss points to suboptimal sample selection rather than beneficial regularization.

List_of_included_plots.description: Validation loss over epochs showing Full Data best, Random intermediate, PADA worst throughout training
List_of_included_plots.analysis: Demonstrates consistent ordering across all epochs: Full Data achieves lowest validation loss, Random Selection is intermediate, and PADA performs worst with final gap of ~0.3 loss units. The persistent gap that doesn't close over training indicates the current phase-aware sampling is systematically selecting less useful samples than even random selection.

Key_numerical_results.description: PADA final validation loss (nodes d595f707, a9d4b83e, 5ebdc33d)
Key_numerical_results.analysis: PADA achieves worse validation loss than both Random Selection (2.6848) and Full Data (2.6512), representing a 10.5% relative degradation compared to Random despite using 52% fewer samples. This indicates the attribution-based selection is not identifying beneficial samples.

Key_numerical_results.description: Total samples used by PADA (nodes d595f707, a9d4b83e, 5ebdc33d)
Key_numerical_results.analysis: PADA uses 28.7% of Full Data's samples (17,200 vs 60,000) and 47.8% of Random's samples (17,200 vs 36,000). While this demonstrates aggressive subsampling, the corresponding validation loss degradation makes this trade-off unfavorable.

Key_numerical_results.description: Total samples used by PADA in alternative implementation (node 0a70d8a1)
Key_numerical_results.analysis: In one implementation variant, PADA paradoxically uses 1.43× MORE samples than Random baseline (88,320 vs 61,890) while achieving worse validation loss (2.5492 vs 2.4918). This reveals inconsistent budget enforcement across implementations.

Key_numerical_results.description: Number of epochs in refinement phase (nodes d595f707, a9d4b83e, 5ebdc33d)
Key_numerical_results.analysis: Phase detector never triggers refinement phase across 20 training epochs, with 5 epochs memorization and 15 epochs generalization. This indicates overly strict refinement criteria, undermining the three-phase design of PADA.

Key_numerical_results.description: Number of epochs in refinement phase (node 0a70d8a1)
Key_numerical_results.analysis: Alternative implementation shows opposite extreme with 18/30 epochs in refinement (6 memorization, 6 generalization). This sensitivity to hyperparameters suggests the phase detection mechanism needs more robust design.

Key_numerical_results.description: Sample efficiency ratio for PADA and Random Selection (final epoch)
Key_numerical_results.analysis: Sample efficiency ratios converge to similar values across methods by end of training, indicating diminishing returns. While PADA shows higher efficiency early (due to using fewer samples), this does not translate to better absolute performance.
